topic: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill
description: Wednesday 5 November 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill Ryan Daniel Avid.legal Limited Murray Whyte, Director Madison Kilworth, Law Clerk Deloitte - GST Allan Bullot, Partner - GST Chartered Accountants Australia New Zealand Craig Elliffe, Tax Advisory Group Chair John Cuthbertson, NZ Tax and Financial Services Leader Max Marshall Tax Justice Aotearoa Glenn Barclay, Chair of Tax Justice Aotearoa Kate Stone, Campaign Manager, Tax Justice Aotearoa Bill Rosenberg, Policy Advisory Committee chair, Tax Justice Aotearoa
descriptionSummary: Taxation (Annual Rates for 2025–26, Compliance Simplification, and Remedial Measures) Bill. Ryan Daniel. Avid.legal Limited

First one is that, uh, the payment of dividends is a liquidity event into the current proposal. Um, the second one is, well, in that regard, um, receiving dividends won't normally amount to the size of the, the tax payment.

Uh, and so a small dividend payment could trigger the, the payment of a, a large ESOP payment. Um, we don't think that's a true liquidity event. Doesn't give an employee the funds to be able to pay the, the tax. Uh, the second one is that it's quite reasonably common for startups, particularly as they grow to, to restructure.

Um, and a typical restructure is what we call a top hat restructure. The employer determines whether the, the, the shares on offer, um, will be considered deferral shares. And we think that should be an employee choice rather than the employer choice. Each employee has their own risk profile, their own individual circumstances then think it would be too hard to put that in the hands of the employee to choose rather than the employer across the, their employee group as a whole.

In the time that we've sat there, the tax debt has gone up to 9.3 billion.

Right? It's gone up over a billion dollars for each of the last two years. If there was a tax loophole in the legislation that was a hundred million dollars, there would be immense action that would be actually happening to close that loophole quickly. This is 10 times bigger than that. Yeah.

There's no point, um, in my mind getting a number on A GST return or PAYE return if it doesn't ultimately turn into cash. And 60% of that tax debt is GST and PAYE, most of which is being held in small medium enterprises. The GST debt has gone up by 15% since last year.

PAYE has gone up by 30%. The real problem, not just from a New Zealand perspective about the loss of revenue to fiscal perspective, but also quoting from inland revenue from their 25 annual report as well as causing the government to lose revenue. Wrongdoing unchecked has a corrosive effect on the morale and willingness of customers who do the right thing voluntary.

And I think that social capital is really critical, particularly there is a massive feeling out there amongst the business community that inland revenue has unwillingly become the first bank of last resort, and they are in effect creating tax zombie companies. And what do we mean by a tax zombie company?

We mean a company that if you are six months behind on your GST and PAYE, you are dead, but you just don't know it. And the problem, I could refer to a parrot, I was advised outside that I should refer to a parrot in the sketch. Um, but you are dead and it's sad.

No one wants a business to fail, but even more, we don't want a business to fail and then linger on and infect other good businesses. And what's really annoying for businesses out there is they see in land revenue as unwillingly being an enabler. Because if you are a plumber working for a contractor, you might advance trade credit, but you'll go to a credit reporting agency and check on the tax debt.

Inland revenue have had the power since 2017 with some pretty well drafted legislation in my view, but had their own internal restrictions that were overly onerous. They have removed those onerous restrictions in the last month or so. So it'll be interesting to see what happens. But last year the debt went up by over a billion dollars and no one was reported to a credit reporting agency.

Now what are we gonna do about it? Because we can't really have Alan sitting down here next year saying, Hey, now we've got 10 and a half or $11 billion. What's actually going to happen? It sounds counterintuitive, but what I'm actually suggesting is I think inland revenue needs to potentially lose its right as a preferential creditor in certain circumstances.

And I can see people go, oh, hang on. Why? What I think is if you get to a period of, say after six months and inland revenue is seen, however we draft the legislation as basically enabling a company to turn into a tax zombie, then if Finland revenue have to report on the fact that they've lost that preferential status, you will drive behavioral change within the department.

There are a lot of good people in the department that've got lots of restrictions and time. But if you drive that behavior, you will also drive behavior out there in the commercial environment. And that should hopefully stop people using inland revenue as the first bank of last resort. So my thought is if it's above whatever it is, x thousand and x hundred thousand or six months, you would lose the preferential status for that six months onwards.

Now inland revenue will lose a little bit of money on that particular creditor, but I think on a population-wide basis, it's got to be better than what we're currently looking at and we just can't go on. Uh, Tina Koto, uh, Mr. Chairman, um, I'm Craig ef. I'm the chairman of the Tax Advisory Group of Charter Accounts Australia, New Zealand. Uh, and John Kaon is the New Zealand Tax and Financial Services leader. Um, chartered accountants represents about 30,000, uh, accountants in New Zealand.

So it's a very large organization. But, um, our, our advice, our, um, our report, which is 119 pages on this bill, um, is not anything but an independent report. Um, and so in other words, we are really interested in the interests of, uh, New Zealand Inc.

Uh, not in the interests necessarily of, um, of individual, uh, accountants. Um, having said that as an introduction, cans, um, basically supports most of the changes, uh, in this bill. Um, and so that is, uh, that's a missed opportunity because you're heading in the right direction. Uh, the problems are that it's too narrow. which is on an unrealized basis, which is, uh, absolutely, um, inappropriate. Right. Uh, I'm, I'm gonna talk about, uh, fringe benefit tax very briefly. It sounds benign, but once you take it out of unclassified benefits, you remove it from the di minimis exemption.

And at the moment, a lot of small employers and even some larger employers will be, um, having benefits of this type in unclassified benefits. And as long as it's less than $300 per employee per quarter or less than 22 and half thousand per employer per annum, they won't be paying French benefit tax on it.

This is a, this is actually quite a significant shift without, I don't know how many people are gonna realize that, but it's important. Uh, then if we move to, uh, trust disclosure rules, we absolutely support the removal of those trust disclosure rules as they stand. And that the rules should be more tailored to what's actually needed. The environment shifted. We now have a 39% tax rate for trustees and for the top marginal rate.

So where's the mischief on some of the stuff? And it comes back ironically to something I talked to you about a couple of years ago on the 39% trustee rate when we were advocating for a 33% rate. We have actually only 11% of trusts in New Zealand that earn over that threshold to be at 39% and they earn 81% of the total trust income.

So there's quite a thing there, and we want to actually have the rules targeted towards those larger trusts, not the smaller trusts in terms of what information you're looking to get. Uh, finally, we would actually support the retention of section 17 gb, which is the information collecting, um, mechanism for tax policy work.
